The Consolidated Provincial Yield Table Chapter 1 Background

The Consolidated Provincial Yield Table Shuyan Jiang, Kelly Izzard, Yingbing Chen 2026-07-23 Chapter 1 Background Forest Analysis and Inventory Branch (FAIB) is in the process of developing and publicizing a single provincial yield dataset that would assign a yield curve to every feature_id in the corporate forest inventory. The approach combines FAIB’s Managed Stand Yield Tables (MSYTs) derived from RESULTS information for harvested and regenerated stands with VDYP-generated natural stand yield curves for unmanaged stands represented in the Vegetation Resources Inventory (VRI). The objective is to establish a comprehensive, wall-to-wall provincial yield framework that can support timber supply analysis, Forest Landscape Planning (FLP), strategic assessments, and other forest management initiatives. One of the primary advantages of a provincial yield dataset is that it would provide complete and consistent coverage across British Columbia. Every forested polygon would have an associated yield projection, eliminating the need for individual projects to assemble yield information independently. This would significantly reduce the effort required to develop inputs for TSRs, FLPs, and provincial strategic analyses while ensuring that all analyses begin from a common and transparent data foundation. A second benefit is improved consistency and comparability across planning units. Currently, yield assumptions often vary between TSAs due to differences in processing methodologies and yield-generation approaches. A standardized provincial dataset promotes a consistent analytical framework across the province, making it easier to compare results among TSAs, evaluate regional trends, and support provincial reporting and decision-making. Such consistency would increase transparency and reduce variability associated with differing technical approaches. The initiative would also maximize the value of existing provincial investments in inventory and growth-and-yield. By integrating RESULTS-based managed stand information, VRI data, VDYP modelling, and existing growth and yield research into a single corporate product, FAIB could leverage datasets that are already maintained and routinely updated. Rather than repeatedly recreating yield packages for individual projects, planners and analysts would be able to access and utilize a common provincial resource that reflects current inventory and management information. Finally, a provincial yield layer would substantially improve analytical efficiency and responsiveness. Having a ready-to-use yield framework would enable rapid evaluation of policy options, disturbance impacts, fibre supply scenarios, and Forest Landscape Planning alternatives. The dataset could become a foundational corporate information asset, much like the VRI itself, supporting a wide range of applications including TSRs, carbon assessments, biodiversity planning, stewardship initiatives, and long-term strategic forest management. By providing a single, authoritative source of yield information, FAIB would strengthen the consistency, scalability, and repeatability of forest analysis across British Columbia. The following details how provincial natural stand yield table (NSYT) component was generated, the amalgamation with the provincial managed stand yield curves into a single provincial current yield table, and the QA assessments of the product.

Chapter 2 Introduction to the Provincial NSYT Production Process

Chapter 2 Introduction to the Provincial NSYT Production Process The Provincial NSYT dataset is a comprehensive province-wide yield product generated from the Rank 1 (R1) layer of the VRI. The R1 layer was selected because it represents the leading forest layer within each polygon and provides a consistent provincial basis for yield estimation. Yield projections were developed using the Variable Density Yield Projection version 7 (VDYP7) and are intended to support strategic forest analysis, TSRs, FLP, cumulative effects assessments, forest estate modelling, carbon analysis, and other provincial forest management applications. The resulting dataset provides a standardized natural stand yield curve for each eligible forest feature in the provincial inventory, creating a common analytical foundation for forest planning and decision-making. Development of the provincial NSYT required extensive preparation and quality assurance of the underlying inputs before yield generation could occur. Input datasets were assembled from provincial VRI, VDYP input layers, VDYP input polygons, species composition records, and supporting tables. A series of validation and correction procedures were implemented to identify and resolve issues related to missing rank assignments, species composition, age and height estimates, basal area, crown closure, density attributes, vegetation cover information, and other inventory anomalies. These steps ensured that the final VDYP inputs were standardized, internally consistent, and suitable for generating reliable provincial-scale yield projections. The following chapters document both the development of the provincial NSYT dataset and the assessments undertaken to evaluate its suitability for operational use. Chapter 3 describes the preparation of the VDYP input database, including data loading, quality assurance procedures, rank layer assignment methodologies, species composition correction, age and height validation, and the updates applied to vegetation and inventory attributes. Chapter 4 summarizes the VDYP projection parameters, assumptions, and model inputs that were ultimately used to generate the final provincial NSYT yield curves. Chapter 5 describes the process used to integrate the Provincial NSYT with the Provincial MSYT dataset, creating a single provincial yield library that includes merchantable volume and dominant height projections for both natural and managed stands. The final chapters focus on validation of the provincial product. Chapter 6 presents a comparison between NSYT-derived projected volumes and the existing live stand volume estimates contained within the VRI. This assessment evaluates the consistency between the new NSYT dataset and the currently published provincial inventory information and helps identify differences arising from age treatments, interpolation methods, yield modelling assumptions, and inventory inputs. Chapter 7 evaluates the implications of using an R1-only projection approach by comparing polygon-level yields generated from the provincial NSYT methodology against yields produced using all eligible forest layers within a polygon. This analysis quantifies the differences between simplified R1-based projections and traditional multi-layer VDYP projections and provides an assessment of the extent to which the provincial NSYT approach reproduces polygon-level yields across the provincial land base. Collectively, these assessments provide confidence that the Provincial NSYT dataset is a robust, transparent, and operationally useful yield product suitable for a wide range of provincial planning and modelling applications.

Chapter 3 VDYP Inputs Preparation 3.1 Load input data 3.2 Process input data

Chapter 3 VDYP Inputs Preparation The following code prepares, validates, and standardizes the provincial 2024 VDYP (Variable Density Yield Prediction) input database prior to yield generation. The workflow begins by establishing a PostgreSQL database connection and loading the core provincial datasets, including the VDYP input layer table, VDYP input polygon table, rank 1 species layer assignments, and the TSA-feature lookup table. These datasets are imported into PostgreSQL where all subsequent processing and quality assurance activities are performed. The overall objective is to create a complete, standardized, and defensible provincial input dataset suitable for generating yield curves for every forested feature in the corporate inventory. The first stage of processing focuses on identifying and resolving structural data issues. The script evaluates features that exist in the VRI-derived rank 1 dataset but are missing from the VDYP polygon table and reviews polygons that do not have a rank 1 forest layer assigned. A standardized hierarchy is then applied to assign rank 1 layers where they are missing. The process prioritizes non-dead layers, crown closure, and layer order to ensure every eligible forested polygon has a representative primary layer for yield modelling. This step establishes consistent layer selection across the province and resolves thousands of missing rank assignments that would otherwise prevent yield generation. The second major component performs a comprehensive cleanup of species composition information for all rank 1 layers. Species records are converted into a standardized format, outdated species and site index codes are corrected, duplicate species entries are aggregated, dominance rankings are rebuilt, and species percentages are normalized to sum to 100 percent. Features containing invalid or missing species information are identified and flagged. All modifications are recorded in a change log to provide full transparency and quality assurance. The resulting output table, prov_updated_spp_2024, provides a province-wide, quality-controlled species composition dataset suitable for VDYP processing. The script then validates and updates basal area, age, and height information used in yield estimation. Missing secondary species ages and heights are populated from the leading species where appropriate, biologically unrealistic values are corrected, and anomalous records such as negative heights or secondary species older than leading species are adjusted. Additional quality assurance checks identify missing layer identifiers, low or missing basal area, missing age or height values, low stem densities, low crown closure values, and excessively old stand ages that may create yield generation problems. All identified issues are recorded and summarized in review tables to support subsequent data correction and verification efforts. Finally, the workflow standardizes other vegetation and land cover attributes contained in the polygon dataset. Missing numeric values are replaced with zeros, missing BEC zone codes are flagged, and vegetation, non-vegetation, and land cover percentages are checked to ensure totals do not exceed 100 percent. Where totals exceed valid limits, proportional scaling factors are applied and documented. The resulting table, prov_updated_other_veg_2024, contains corrected cover attributes, scaling ratios, and a complete audit trail of all adjustments. Collectively, these processing steps create a robust provincial VDYP input database that supports consistent yield generation and forms the foundation for developing a single provincial yield curve dataset for every feature_id in the corporate forest inventory. 3.1 Load input data 3.1.1 Postgres database connection library(RPostgreSQL) library(data.table) library(tidyverse) library(readr) library(kableExtra) # simple connection # SCHEMA are referenced explicitly in the table names user_name <- 'postgres' data_base <- 'vdyp' con <- dbConnect('PostgreSQL',dbname=data_base,user=user_name) kablep <- function (x) { kable(x,format='markdown') } # set this up as a function run_sql <- function(fn){ system2("psql",args=c('-d',data_base,'-U',user_name,'-f',fn)) } SQLQuery <- function(query){ con <- dbConnect('PostgreSQL',dbname=data_base,user=user_name) data <- dbGetQuery(con,query) dbDisconnect(con) return(data) } 3.1.2 Load 2024 VDYP input layer table to postgres The Provincial VDYP Input Layer is the foundational stand-level dataset used by the Variable Density Yield Projection system version 7 (VDYP7) to generate natural stand yield projections for the Provincial Natural Stand Yield Table (NSYT). Each record represents an individual forest layer within a VRI polygon and contains the inventory attributes required to construct a VDYP7 stand description, including species composition, species percentages, age, height, basal area, stems per hectare, crown closure, site index, layer classification, and biogeoclimatic information. The dataset is derived from the Provincial Vegetation Resources Inventory (VRI) and is structured to support the VDYP7 startup processes, particularly VRISTART, which transforms photo-interpreted inventory attributes into the stand descriptions used for growth and yield projection. The input layer therefore provides the critical biological and structural information needed to estimate current stand conditions and future yield development. For the Provincial NSYT initiative, the input layer was used to identify and prepare the Rank 1 (R1) forest layer for each feature, representing the leading canopy layer within a polygon. Extensive quality assurance procedures were applied to ensure that species composition, age, height, density, basal area, crown closure, and site index information were internally consistent and suitable for modelling. Because VDYP7 relies heavily on attributes such as basal area, stems per hectare, species mixtures, site productivity, and stand age to construct and project stand descriptions, input data quality directly influences projected yields. The resulting Provincial VDYP Input Layer provides a standardized, province-wide inventory foundation that enables consistent yield generation across millions of forested features and supports the development of a comprehensive provincial natural stand yield framework. file_path <- "D:/data/projects/VDYP/VDYP/2024/vdyp_input_layer_2024.csv" file <- read_csv(file_path) colnames(file) <- tolower(colnames(file)) dbWriteTable(con, "vdyp7_input_layer_2024", file, row.names=FALSE, overwrite=TRUE) rm(file) 3.1.3 Load 2024 VDYP input polygon table to postgres The VDYP Polygon Table is the polygon-level companion dataset to the VDYP Input Layer table and provides the non-tree and site-level attributes required by VDYP7 to characterize each VRI feature. While the VDYP Input Layer table contains detailed forest layer information such as species composition, age, height, basal area, stems per hectare, and crown closure, the Polygon Table contains attributes that describe the overall polygon, including its area, ecological classification, vegetation cover, non-vegetated cover, land cover composition, disturbance history, and inventory metadata. Together, the layer and polygon tables provide a complete description of each forest feature required for VDYP processing. Within the Provincial NSYT workflow, the Polygon Table is used primarily to provide the ecological and land-cover context for each feature. BEC zone information is particularly important because VDYP7 applies different growth, yield, taper, decay, waste, breakage, and volume relationships depending on ecological region. Vegetation and land-cover percentages are also reviewed and standardized to ensure the combined cover values are logically consistent and do not exceed 100 percent. As part of the provincial preprocessing workflow, missing BEC codes are flagged, missing vegetation values are replaced with zeros, and vegetation, non-vegetation, and land-cover percentages are proportionally scaled when totals exceed valid limits. These quality-control procedures produce the prov_updated_other_veg_2024 table used during provincial yield generation. file_path <- "D:/data/projects/VDYP/VDYP/2024/vdyp_input_poly_2024.csv" file <- read_csv(file_path) colnames(file) <- tolower(colnames(file)) dbWriteTable(con, "vdyp7_input_poly_2024", file, row.names=FALSE, overwrite=TRUE) rm(file) 3.1.4 Load rank1 2024 The Rank 1 (R1) layer represents the primary forest canopy layer within a VRI polygon and serves as the principal stand description used for most provincial forest inventory analyses. A VRI polygon may contain multiple layers, such as a primary (P) layer, residual (R) layer, veteran (V) layer, or dead (D) layer; however, the R1 layer identifies the dominant live forest layer that best characterizes the stand. The R1 record contains the key stand attributes required for growth and yield modelling, including leading and secondary species composition, species percentages, estimated age, dominant height, site index, basal area, stems per hectare, crown closure, and adjusted volume estimates. These attributes are derived through the VRI interpretation process and subsequent VDYP adjustment procedures and provide a standardized representation of the forest stand across the provincial land base. For the Provincial Natural Stand Yield Table (NSYT) project, the R1 layer was selected as the sole input layer for yield generation because it represents the dominant forest condition within each feature and provides a consistent province-wide basis for modelling. Using the R1 layer simplifies the yield generation process while retaining the stand attributes that have the greatest influence on forest productivity and timber volume. file_path <- "D:/data/projects/VDYP/VDYP/2024/rank1_2024.csv" file <- read_csv(file_path) names(file) <- tolower(names(file)) dbWriteTable(con, "rank1_2024", file, row.names=FALSE, overwrite=TRUE) rm(file) 3.1.5 where feature_id not in (select distinct feature_id from vdyp7_input_layer_2024 where rank_cd_update = 1) group by 1') kablep(r1) 3.2.3 Check & fix species comp for rank1 3.2.3.1 Issues Addressed species_long_cd, species_long_pct, by = c("feature_id", "rank") ) # ----------------------------------------------- # Remove invalid species codes # ----------------------------------------------- spp_comp$feature_id %in% changed_codes$feature_id ], "SPECIES_CODE_UPDATED" ) } species_long <- species_long %>% select(-original_cd) # ----------------------------------------------- # Aggregate <- species_long %>% group_by(feature_id, species_cd) %>% summarise(total_percentage = sum(percentage), .groups = "drop") # ----------------------------------------------- # Order species by descending df_merged$sum_pct < 100 & df_merged$sum_pct > 0 if (any(idx)) { diff_needed <- 100 - df_merged$sum_pct[idx] df_merged$species_pct_1[idx] <- df_merged$species_pct_1[idx] + diff_needed 100 idx <- df_merged$sum_pct > 100 if (any(idx)) { scale_factor <- 100 / df_merged$sum_pct[idx] df_merged[idx, pct_cols] <- df_merged[idx, pct_cols] * scale_factor spp_comp$change_log[ ----------------------------------------------- # If no changes occurred # ----------------------------------------------- layer_attri$change_log[is.na(layer_attri$change_log)] <- "NO_ADJUSTMENT" dbWriteTable(con, "prov_updated_ba_age_ht_2024", layer_attri, overwrite = TRUE, row.names = FALSE) 3.2.5 Review Basal Area, Age & Height 3.2.5.1 Checks Performed Missing layer identifiers: layer_level_code is NULL vdyp7_layer_cd is NULL Basal Area issues: basal_area_75 is NULL or < 1 Age & Height issues: est_age_spp1 or est_height_spp1 is NULL est_age_spp1 > 250 (old stands, may not have yields generated) species_pct_2 > 0 but age/height missing density issues: stems_per_ha_75 is NULL or < 1 Crown closure issues: crown_closure is NULL or < 1 3.2.5.2 Output Table: prov_ba_age_ht_checks_2024 Includes all original fields plus change_log describing detected issues library(tidyr) library(dplyr) query <- "SELECT feature_id,layer_level_code, vdyp7_layer_cd, species_cd_1, species_cd_2, species_pct_2, basal_area_75, stems_per_ha_75, crown_closure, est_age_spp1,est_height_spp1,est_age_spp2,est_height_spp2 FROM vdyp7_input_layer_2024 where rank_cd_update = 1;" layer_attri <- dbGetQuery(con, query) # ----------------------------------------------- # Initialize change tracking field # ----------------------------------------------- layer_attri$change_log <- NA_character_ add_change <- function(log, tag) { ifelse( is.na(log), tag, paste(log, tag, sep = " | ") ) } # layer level code is missing idx <- is.na(layer_attri$layer_level_code) layer_attri$change_log[idx] <- add_change(layer_attri$change_log[idx], "missing layer level code") # vdyp7_layer_cd is missing idx <- is.na(layer_attri$vdyp7_layer_cd) layer_attri$change_log[idx] <- add_change(layer_attri$change_log[idx], "missing vdyp7_layer_cd") # species_cd_1 is missing idx <- is.na(layer_attri$species_cd_1) layer_attri$change_log[idx] <- add_change(layer_attri$change_log[idx], "missing species_cd_1") # 1. Copy age1 → age2 if age2 missing & species_pct_2 > 0 idx <- !is.na(layer_attri$species_pct_2) & layer_attri$species_pct_2 > 0 & (is.na(layer_attri$est_age_spp2) | layer_attri$est_age_spp2 == 0) & !is.na(layer_attri$est_age_spp1) & layer_attri$est_age_spp1 > 0 layer_attri$change_log[idx] <- add_change(layer_attri$change_log[idx], "missing est_age_spp2") idx <- !is.na(layer_attri$species_pct_2) & layer_attri$species_pct_2 > 0 & (is.na(layer_attri$est_height_spp2) | layer_attri$est_height_spp2 == 0) & !is.na(layer_attri$est_height_spp1) & layer_attri$est_height_spp1 > 0 layer_attri$change_log[idx] <- add_change(layer_attri$change_log[idx], "missing est_height_spp2") # 2. Fix age2 > age1 → copy age1 idx <- !is.na(layer_attri$est_age_spp2) & !is.na(layer_attri$est_age_spp1) & layer_attri$est_age_spp2 > layer_attri$est_age_spp1 layer_attri$change_log[idx] <- add_change(layer_attri$change_log[idx], "est_age_spp2 > est_age_spp1") idx <- !is.na(layer_attri$est_height_spp2) & !is.na(layer_attri$est_height_spp1) & layer_attri$est_height_spp2 > layer_attri$est_height_spp1 layer_attri$change_log[idx] <- add_change(layer_attri$change_log[idx], "est_height_spp2 > est_height_spp1") # 4. Replace height2 = -1 → 0 idx <- !is.na(layer_attri$est_height_spp2) & layer_attri$est_height_spp2 == -1 layer_attri$change_log[idx] <- add_change(layer_attri$change_log[idx], "est_height_spp2 < 0") # Basal Area < 1 idx <- is.na(layer_attri$basal_area_75) | layer_attri$basal_area_75 < 1 layer_attri$change_log[idx] <- add_change(layer_attri$change_log[idx], "BA null or lower than 1") # age1 or height 1 is null idx <- !is.na(layer_attri$species_cd_1) & is.na(layer_attri$est_height_spp1) layer_attri$change_log[idx] <- add_change(layer_attri$change_log[idx], "missing est_height_spp1") idx <- !is.na(layer_attri$species_cd_1) & is.na(layer_attri$est_age_spp1) layer_attri$change_log[idx] <- add_change(layer_attri$change_log[idx], "missing est_age_spp1") # # having spp2 but age2 or height 2 is null idx <- !is.na(layer_attri$species_pct_2) &layer_attri$species_pct_2 > 0 & (is.na(layer_attri$est_height_spp2)| layer_attri$est_height_spp2 == 0 | is.na(layer_attri$est_age_spp2)| layer_attri$est_age_spp2 == 0) layer_attri$change_log[idx] <- add_change(layer_attri$change_log[idx], "missing age2 or ht2") # old age idx <- !is.na(layer_attri$est_age_spp1) & layer_attri$est_age_spp1 >= 250 layer_attri$change_log[idx] <- add_change(layer_attri$change_log[idx], "old age > 250") # SPH null idx <- is.na(layer_attri$stems_per_ha_75) | layer_attri$stems_per_ha_75 < 1 layer_attri$change_log[idx] <- add_change(layer_attri$change_log[idx], "stems_per_ha_75 null or lower than 1") # ----------------------------------------------- # Initialize change tracking field # ----------------------------------------------- df$change_log <- NA_character_ add_change <- function(log, tag) { ifelse( is.na(log), tag, paste(log, tag, sep = " | ") ) } # ----------------------------------------------- # No Layer # ----------------------------------------------- no_layer_idx <- df$feature_id %in% no_layer$feature_id if (any(no_layer_idx)) { df$change_log[no_layer_idx] <- add_change(df$change_log[no_layer_idx], "NO_LAYER") } # ----------------------------------------------- # Check missing BEC zone # ----------------------------------------------- bec_missing_idx <- is.na(df$bec_zone_code) | trimws(df$bec_zone_code) == "" if (any(bec_missing_idx)) { df$change_log[bec_missing_idx] <- add_change(df$change_log[bec_missing_idx], "MISSING_BEC") } # ----------------------------------------------- # Replace numeric NA with 0 # ----------------------------------------------- numeric_cols <- setdiff(names(df), c("feature_id", "bec_zone_code", "change_log")) df[, numeric_cols][is.na(df[, numeric_cols])] <- 0 # ----------------------------------------------- # Vegetation Layer Scaling # ----------------------------------------------- df$total_veg <- df$shrub_crown_closure + df$herb_cover_pct + df$bryoid_cover_pct veg_idx <- df$total_veg > 100 if (any(veg_idx)) { ratio <- 100 / df$total_veg[veg_idx] df$shrub_crown_closure[veg_idx] <- floor(df$shrub_crown_closure[veg_idx] * ratio) df$herb_cover_pct[veg_idx] <- floor(df$herb_cover_pct[veg_idx] * ratio) df$bryoid_cover_pct[veg_idx] <- floor(df$bryoid_cover_pct[veg_idx] * ratio) df$change_log[veg_idx] <- add_change(df$change_log[veg_idx], "VEG_SCALED_DOWN") } df$ratio_veg <- ifelse(df$total_veg > 100, 100 / df$total_veg, 1) # ----------------------------------------------- # Non-Vegetated Cover Scaling # ----------------------------------------------- df$total_non_veg <- df$non_veg_cover_pct_1 + df$non_veg_cover_pct_2 + df$non_veg_cover_pct_3 nonveg_idx <- df$total_non_veg > 100 if (any(nonveg_idx)) { ratio <- 100 / df$total_non_veg[nonveg_idx] df$non_veg_cover_pct_1[nonveg_idx] <- floor(df$non_veg_cover_pct_1[nonveg_idx] * ratio) df$non_veg_cover_pct_2[nonveg_idx] <- floor(df$non_veg_cover_pct_2[nonveg_idx] * ratio) df$non_veg_cover_pct_3[nonveg_idx] <- floor(df$non_veg_cover_pct_3[nonveg_idx] * ratio) df$change_log[nonveg_idx] <- add_change(df$change_log[nonveg_idx], "NONVEG_SCALED_DOWN") } df$ratio_non_veg <- ifelse(df$total_non_veg > 100, 100 / df$total_non_veg, 1) # ----------------------------------------------- # Land Cover Scaling # ----------------------------------------------- df$total_land <- df$land_cover_pct_1 + df$land_cover_pct_2 + df$land_cover_pct_3 land_idx <- df$total_land > 100 if (any(land_idx)) { ratio <- 100 / df$total_land[land_idx] df$land_cover_pct_1[land_idx] <- floor(df$land_cover_pct_1[land_idx] * ratio) df$land_cover_pct_2[land_idx] <- floor(df$land_cover_pct_2[land_idx] * ratio) df$land_cover_pct_3[land_idx] <- floor(df$land_cover_pct_3[land_idx] * ratio) df$change_log[land_idx] <- add_change(df$change_log[land_idx], "LANDCOVER_SCALED_DOWN") } df$ratio_land <- ifelse(df$total_land > 100, 100 / df$total_land, 1) # ----------------------------------------------- # If no changes occurred # ----------------------------------------------- df$change_log[is.na(df$change_log)] <- "NO_ADJUSTMENT" # ----------------------------------------------- # Final Output # ----------------------------------------------- updated_df <- df %>% select( feature_id, bec_zone_code, shrub_crown_closure, herb_cover_pct, bryoid_cover_pct, non_veg_cover_pct_1, non_veg_cover_pct_2, non_veg_cover_pct_3, land_cover_pct_1, land_cover_pct_2, land_cover_pct_3, total_veg, ratio_veg, total_non_veg, ratio_non_veg, total_land, ratio_land, change_log ) dbWriteTable( con, "prov_updated_other_veg_2024", updated_df, overwrite = TRUE, row.names = FALSE )

Chapter 4 Run VDYP7 Projection 4.1 VDYPinR 4.2 Load and finalize VDYP input layer data 4.3 Load and finalize VDYP input polygon data 4.4 Set utilization 4.5 Run VDYP7 in parallel

Chapter 4 Run VDYP7 Projection VDYP7 console version: 7.17d projection parameters: parameters value time series age 0-250 years old time increments 10 years reported utilization level 12.5cm pine and deciduous, 17.5cm for all other species 4.1 VDYPinR VDYP7 projections were performed using VDYPinR package in the R environment. VDYPinR is an R package developed to provide a direct interface between the R statistical environment and VDYP7console interface from VDYP7 system. The package executes VDYP7 simulations, and imports the resulting yield tables directly back into R for further analysis. It was designed to simplify large-scale forest modelling workflows by eliminating the need to manually create VDYP7 command files, manage intermediate files, or process outputs outside of R. The package includes functions to validate input datasets, generate utilization tables, run VDYP7 simulations, extract volume and biomass outputs, and save results in multiple formats. Within the Provincial NSYT process, VDYPinR serves as the primary processing framework used to generate yield projections for the provincial inventory. After the VDYP polygon and layer inputs are prepared and validated, VDYPinR partitions the provincial dataset into manageable batches, submits these records to the VDYP7Console engine, and assembles the resulting yield tables for further analysis. The package therefore provides the operational bridge between provincial inventory data and the underlying VDYP7 growth model, allowing large-scale, reproducible, and automated yield generation entirely within an R workflow. This capability was essential for producing a province-wide NSYT dataset covering millions of forest features while maintaining consistency with the standard VDYP7 modelling framework used throughout British Columbia. 4.2 Load and finalize VDYP input layer data This step extracts, standardizes, and prepares the final VDYP7 layer input dataset that is used to generate provincial NSYT projections. The script begins by establishing a connection to the PostgreSQL VDYP database and then retrieves all records associated with the validated Rank 1 (R1) forest layers. During extraction, the script joins the original VDYP input layer table with the updated species composition table (prov_updated_spp_2024) and the updated age and height table (prov_updated_ba_age_ht_2024) to incorporate all corrections and quality assurance updates completed during pre-processing. The resulting dataset contains the complete set of VDYP7 stand attributes required by the VRISTART process, including species composition, site index, age, height, basal area, crown closure, stems per hectare, volume attributes, and layer descriptors. The non_forest_descriptor_code field is intentionally populated as blank, and only records where the updated rank code equals one are retained, ensuring that yield projections are generated exclusively for the leading forest layer within each feature. Following extraction, the dataset is standardized by converting field names to uppercase, sorting records by FEATURE_ID, and storing the complete provincial layer dataset as an RDS file for subsequent processing. To support efficient large-scale yield generation using VDYP7 Console, the provincial dataset is then divided into fifty approximately equal-sized groups based on FEATURE_ID. These partitions enable parallel processing across multiple VDYP7 runs, substantially reducing execution time when generating yield curves for millions of forest features. The resulting collection of partitioned input files forms the final R1-only provincial VDYP input layer dataset used to create the Provincial NSYT yield tables. library(RPostgreSQL) library(VDYPinR) library(dplyr) # simple connection # SCHEMA are referenced explicitly in the table names user_name <- 'postgres' data_base <- 'vdyp' con <- dbConnect('PostgreSQL',dbname=data_base,user=user_name) query <- "SELECT l.feature_id, tree_cover_layer_estimated_id, map_id, polygon_number, layer_level_code_update as layer_level_code, vdyp7_layer_cd, layer_stockability, rank_cd_update as forest_cover_rank_code, '' as non_forest_descriptor_code, est_site_index_species_cd, estimated_site_index, crown_closure, basal_area_75, stems_per_ha_75, n.species_cd_1, n.species_pct_1, n.species_cd_2, n.species_pct_2, n.species_cd_3, n.species_pct_3, n.species_cd_4, n.species_pct_4, n.species_cd_5, n.species_pct_5, n.species_cd_6, n.species_pct_6, k.est_age_spp1, k.est_height_spp1, k.est_age_spp2, k.est_height_spp2, adj_ind, lorey_height_75, basal_area_125, ws_vol_per_ha_75, ws_vol_per_ha_125, cu_vol_per_ha_125, d_vol_per_ha_125, dw_vol_per_ha_125 FROM public.vdyp7_input_layer_2024 l LEFT JOIN prov_updated_spp_2024 n ON l.feature_id = n.feature_id LEFT JOIN prov_updated_ba_age_ht_2024 k ON l.feature_id = k.feature_id where rank_cd_update = 1" layer <- dbGetQuery(con, query) colnames(layer) <- toupper(colnames(layer)) layer <- layer %>% mutate(FEATURE_ID = as.integer(FEATURE_ID)) layer <- layer %>% arrange(FEATURE_ID) saveRDS(layer, file = "D:/data/projects/VDYP/VDYP/2024/prov/VDYP7RunParallel/layer.rds") layer <- layer %>% mutate(group = ntile(FEATURE_ID, 50)) table(layer$group) layer_parts <- split(layer, layer$group) head(layer_parts[[1]]) 4.3 Load and finalize VDYP input polygon data The Provincial VDYP Polygon Input dataset was prepared by extracting all polygon-level records associated with features that contained a valid Rank 1 (R1) forest layer and joining them with the quality-controlled vegetation cover updates developed during the preprocessing phase. The workflow combined the original vdyp7_input_poly_2024 table with the corrected prov_updated_other_veg_2024 table, ensuring that all adjustments applied to shrub cover, herb cover, bryoid cover, non-vegetated cover, and land-cover percentages were incorporated into the final VDYP input file. The resulting dataset retained the full set of VDYP7 polygon attributes required for projection, including polygon identifiers, inventory standard codes, ecological classifications, BEC zone information, stockability, yield factors, disturbance information, BCLCS land-cover classifications, vegetation cover estimates, non-vegetated cover components, and land-cover composition attributes. Only polygons with an associated R1 layer selected for projection were included, ensuring that the final polygon input dataset corresponded directly to the provincial NSYT feature set. Following extraction and assembly, the polygon records were standardized by converting field names to uppercase, converting FEATURE_ID to an integer format, and sorting all records by FEATURE_ID to satisfy VDYP7 input requirements. The completed provincial polygon table was then saved as an RDS file and linked to the same processing groups previously created for the VDYP layer input dataset. By inheriting the grouping structure used for the layer table, each polygon record was assigned to one of fifty approximately equal-sized processing partitions. The provincial polygon dataset was subsequently split into these individual subsets, allowing the polygon and layer inputs to be processed together in parallel using the VDYP7inR framework and VDYP7 Console. This partitioning strategy enabled efficient large-scale yield generation across millions of provincial forest features while maintaining the one-to-one relationship between VDYP polygon and layer input records required by the model. query <- "SELECT l.feature_id, map_id, polygon_number, org_unit, tsa_name, tfl_name, inventory_standard_code, tsa_number, shrub_height, n.shrub_crown_closure, shrub_cover_pattern, herb_cover_type_code, n.herb_cover_pct, herb_cover_pattern_code, n.bryoid_cover_pct, l.bec_zone_code, cfs_ecozone, pre_disturbance_stockability, yield_factor, non_productive_descriptor_cd, bclcs_level1_code, bclcs_level2_code, bclcs_level3_code, bclcs_level4_code, bclcs_level5_code, photo_estimation_base_year, reference_year, pct_dead, non_veg_cover_type_1, n.non_veg_cover_pct_1, non_veg_cover_pattern_1, non_veg_cover_type_2, n.non_veg_cover_pct_2, non_veg_cover_pattern_2, non_veg_cover_type_3, n.non_veg_cover_pct_3, non_veg_cover_pattern_3, land_cover_class_cd_1, n.land_cover_pct_1, land_cover_class_cd_2, n.land_cover_pct_2, land_cover_class_cd_3, n.land_cover_pct_3 FROM public.vdyp7_input_poly_2024 l LEFT JOIN prov_updated_other_veg_2024 n ON l.feature_id = n.feature_id where l.feature_id in (select distinct feature_id from vdyp7_input_layer_2024 where rank_cd_update = 1)" polygon <- dbGetQuery(con, query) colnames(polygon) <- toupper(colnames(polygon)) polygon <- polygon %>% mutate(FEATURE_ID = as.integer(FEATURE_ID)) polygon <- polygon %>% arrange(FEATURE_ID) saveRDS(polygon, file = "D:/data/projects/VDYP/VDYP/2024/prov/VDYP7RunParallel/polygon.rds") polygon_groups <- layer %>% select(FEATURE_ID, group) polygon <- polygon %>% left_join(polygon_groups, by = "FEATURE_ID") table(polygon$group) polygon_parts <- split(polygon, polygon$group) head(polygon_parts[[1]]) 4.4 Set utilization Before running the provincial VDYP7 projections, all polygon and layer input files were validated using the validInput() function from the VDYPinR package to ensure that the polygon and layer datasets were structurally correct, internally consistent, and suitable for processing by VDYP7. Validation was performed independently on each of the fifty processing partitions created during data preparation, allowing any mismatches between polygon and layer records, missing required attributes, or formatting issues to be identified before yield generation commenced. Following successful validation, a provincial utilization table was created using utilTableGenerator() to define the minimum merchantable diameter thresholds applied during volume calculations. Under this configuration, all deciduous species groups and all pine species groups (lodgepole pine, ponderosa pine, whitebark pine, and western white pine), were assigned a utilization limit of 12.5 cm DBH, while all remaining conifer species groups were assigned a utilization limit of 17.5 cm DBH. This utilization table was subsequently supplied to the VDYP7 modelling process and controlled the merchantability standards used when generating provincial yield projections and summary volume estimates. library(VDYPinR) for (i in 1:50) { validInput(polyFile = polygon_parts[[i]], layerFile = layer_parts[[i]]) } utiltbl <- utilTableGenerator(all=17.5,exception = c("PW,12.5","PA,12.5","PL,12.5","PY,12.5","MB,12.5","D, 12.5", "AC,12.5","AT,12.5","E,12.5")) print(utiltbl) 4.5 Run VDYP7 in parallel Following preparation and validation of the provincial VDYP polygon and layer input datasets, yield projections were generated using the VDYPinR parallel-processing framework to improve computational efficiency and manage the large volume of records being processed. For each group, the VDYP7RunParallel() function automatically prepared VDYP7 input files, executed the VDYP7 Console model, and imported the resulting yield tables back into R. Projections were generated on an age basis from age 0 to age 250 in 10-year intervals, producing a complete yield curve for each feature. The previously defined utilization standards were applied during projection, and reference-year records were not explicitly forced into the output, resulting in a consistent set of age-based natural stand yield tables across the provincial inventory. As each processing group completed, the resulting yield tables, processing outputs, and associated metadata were saved to a provincial output directory using the VDYP7Save() function. Metadata files were retained to provide a complete record of the VDYP7 command settings and processing specifications used for each run, supporting transparency, reproducibility, and troubleshooting if required. The workflow also recorded the start time, completion time, and total execution time in a log file, allowing overall processing performance to be monitored throughout the provincial run. Once all fifty processing groups were completed, the collection of yield tables formed the provincial NSYT output dataset. # Store results in a list results <- vector("list", length = 50) targetfolder <- "D:\\data\\projects\\VDYP\\VDYP\\2024\\prov\\VDYP7RunParallel" log_file <- "D:\\data\\projects\\VDYP\\VDYP\\2024\\prov\\VDYP7RunParallel\\run_times.log" # Loop through all groups start_time <- Sys.time() write(paste("Run started at:", start_time), file = log_file, append = TRUE) for (i in 1:50) { proj_vol <- VDYP7RunParallel(polyFile = polygon_parts[[i]], layerFile = layer_parts[[i]], utiltbl, VDYP7consolePath = "C:/Program Files/VDYP7", # depends on where you install your VDYP7 timeSeries = "age", startTime = 0, endTime = 250, timeIncrement = 10, forceRefYear = FALSE) VDYP7Save(proj_vol, savePath = targetfolder, saveName = paste("prov_lesschanges",i), overWrite = TRUE, metadata = TRUE) dir(targetfolder) } end_time <- Sys.time() total_time <- round(difftime(end_time, start_time, units = "secs"), 2) write(paste("Total execution time:", total_time, "seconds"), file = log_file, append = TRUE) write(paste("Run ended at:", end_time), file = log_file, append = TRUE)

Chapter 5 Consolidated Provincial Yield Table - NSYT and MSYT Combined Table 5.1 Overview 5.2 Build MSYT component 5.3 Build NSYT component 5.4 Combine NSYT tables and MSYT tables and export 5.5 QA/QC checks

Chapter 5 Consolidated Provincial Yield Table - NSYT and MSYT Combined Table 5.1 Overview The following creates a single provincial yield table by integrating both NSYT generated through the VDYP workflow and MSYT generated through the provincial MSYT process. The objective is to produce a standardized yield dataset for every forested feature_id and opening in the provincial inventory, providing merchantable volume and dominant height projections across a common age range. The final product contains age-based estimates for conifer and deciduous merchantable volume (MVcon and MVdec) as well as conifer and deciduous dominant height (HTcon and HTdec), creating a unified yield layer that can be used in timber supply analysis, forest estate modelling, carbon assessments, and other initiatives. The first component of the workflow processes the VDYP-generated natural stand yield projections. Species composition information is extracted from the VDYP yield tables and evaluated against a predefined list of deciduous species. A custom function separates projected merchantable volume into conifer and deciduous components by removing deciduous species volumes from the total projected volume. Age-specific projections are then converted from a long format into a wide yield table where each age step becomes a separate volume field. This process creates a complete set of conifer and deciduous merchantable volume projections for all natural stands represented by the VDYP yield system. The second component processes the provincial MSYT managed stand yield database. Existing MSYT outputs are loaded together with reference tables identifying which features use managed stand yields. Because MSYT curves are typically generated to age 120, the script extends all volume and height projections to age 250 by carrying forward age-120 values for older ages. The resulting managed stand yield curves are standardized into the same structure as the VDYP outputs, ensuring that merchantable volume and dominant height fields are available at identical age intervals and can be combined seamlessly with natural stand projections. To ensure consistency across both yield systems, the script also develops conifer and deciduous dominant height projections for VDYP stands. Using BEC-specific height ratio tables, dominant heights are separated into conifer and deciduous components based on the leading species and associated ecological region. The processed VDYP yields are then combined with the MSYT yields into a single provincial output table. Finally, quality assurance checks identify any records that contain zero merchantable volume across all age classes, allowing both NSYT and MSYT records to be reviewed for potential data issues before the final combined yield table is exported. The resulting dataset provides a comprehensive provincial yield library that supports a consistent yield curve for every feature_id in the corporate inventory and establishes a common foundation for provincial-scale forest analysis and modelling. This script generates a combined yield table containing: Conifer merchantable volume (MVcon_*) Deciduous merchantable volume (MVdec_*) Conifer dominant height (HTcon_*) Deciduous dominant height (HTdec_*) The workflow combines: VDYP-based yield curves (NSYT workflow) MSYT yield curves The final output is exported as a single yield table for downstream modeling. ## Load Libraries library(data.table) library(dplyr) library(tidyr) 5.2 Build MSYT component 5.2.1 Load MSYT tables msyt <- read.csv( "D://data//projects//MSYTDelivery//msyt_2025//prov//MSYT_prov_current_input_output.csv" ) reference <- read.csv( "D://data//projects//MSYTDelivery//msyt_2025//prov//MSYT_prov_reference.csv" ) msyt_fut_in <- read.csv( "D://data//projects//MSYTDelivery//msyt_2025//prov//MSYT_prov_future_input.csv" ) 5.2.2 Extend MSYT curves to age 250 Ages greater than 120 are populated using age-120 values. new_ages <- seq(130, 250, 10) msyt_expanded <- msyt for (age in new_ages) { msyt_expanded <- msyt_expanded %>% mutate( !!paste0("MVcon_", age) := MVcon_120, !!paste0("MVdec_", age) := MVdec_120, !!paste0("HTcon_", age) := HTcon_120, !!paste0("HTdec_", age) := HTdec_120 ) } 5.2.3 Reorder MSYT columns ages <- seq(0, 250, 10) ordered_cols <- c( "feature_id", paste0("MVcon_", ages), paste0("MVdec_", ages), paste0("HTcon_", ages), paste0("HTdec_", ages) ) msyt_expanded <- msyt_expanded %>% select(all_of(ordered_cols)) 5.2.4 Finalize MSYT output msyt_part <- reference %>% filter(!current_yield %in% c("VDYP", "Excluded")) %>% select("feature_id") %>% left_join(msyt_expanded, by = "feature_id") %>% mutate(source = "MSYT") 5.3 Build NSYT component 5.3.1 Load VDYP yield table yld <- readRDS( "D:\\data\\projects\\VDYP\\VDYP\\2024\\prov\\VDYP7RunParallel\\prov_lesschanges_nsyt_yieldtable.rds" ) setDT(yld) 5.3.2 Separate conifer volume and deciduous volume 5.3.2.1 Define deciduous species Species in this list are treated as deciduous decid <- c( "AC","ACB","ACT","AT","AD","AX", "DR","E","EA","EB","EE","EP","ES","EW", "EXP","XH","G","GP","KC","M","MB","MN","MV","Q","QG","XH", "WT","WS","WP","WD","WB","WA","W", "VP","VB","V","U","RA" ) NoDecidVol_calculator() calculates conifer-only projected volume by removing deciduous species from VDYP projected species volumes. NoDecidVol_calculator <- function (sp_list, vol_list, decidsp, multilayer = FALSE) { # Standardize species slot names sp_list[spcode %in% "SPECIES_1_CODE", `:=`(spcode, "sp1")] sp_list[spcode %in% "SPECIES_2_CODE", `:=`(spcode, "sp2")] sp_list[spcode %in% "SPECIES_3_CODE", `:=`(spcode, "sp3")] sp_list[spcode %in% "SPECIES_4_CODE", `:=`(spcode, "sp4")] sp_list[spcode %in% "SPECIES_5_CODE", `:=`(spcode, "sp5")] sp_list[spcode %in% "SPECIES_6_CODE", `:=`(spcode, "sp6")] # Remove deciduous species sp_list2 <- sp_list[ !is.na(sp) & sp != "" & !sp %in% decidsp ] # Replace NA projected volumes with 0 vol_list[is.na(PRJ_SP1_VOL_DWB), `:=`(PRJ_SP1_VOL_DWB, 0)] vol_list[is.na(PRJ_SP2_VOL_DWB), `:=`(PRJ_SP2_VOL_DWB, 0)] vol_list[is.na(PRJ_SP3_VOL_DWB), `:=`(PRJ_SP3_VOL_DWB, 0)] vol_list[is.na(PRJ_SP4_VOL_DWB), `:=`(PRJ_SP4_VOL_DWB, 0)] vol_list[is.na(PRJ_SP5_VOL_DWB), `:=`(PRJ_SP5_VOL_DWB, 0)] vol_list[is.na(PRJ_SP6_VOL_DWB), `:=`(PRJ_SP6_VOL_DWB, 0)] # Convert projected species volumes to long format vol_list2 <- melt( vol_list, id.vars = c("FEATURE_ID", "PRJ_TOTAL_AGE"), measure.vars = c( "PRJ_SP1_VOL_DWB", "PRJ_SP2_VOL_DWB", "PRJ_SP3_VOL_DWB", "PRJ_SP4_VOL_DWB", "PRJ_SP5_VOL_DWB", "PRJ_SP6_VOL_DWB" ), variable.name = "spcode", value.name = "vol" ) # Standardize projected volume species names vol_list2[spcode %in% "PRJ_SP1_VOL_DWB", `:=`(spcode, "sp1")] vol_list2[spcode %in% "PRJ_SP2_VOL_DWB", `:=`(spcode, "sp2")] vol_list2[spcode %in% "PRJ_SP3_VOL_DWB", `:=`(spcode, "sp3")] vol_list2[spcode %in% "PRJ_SP4_VOL_DWB", `:=`(spcode, "sp4")] vol_list2[spcode %in% "PRJ_SP5_VOL_DWB", `:=`(spcode, "sp5")] vol_list2[spcode %in% "PRJ_SP6_VOL_DWB", `:=`(spcode, "sp6")] # Merge species and volume tables all <- merge( sp_list2, vol_list2, by = c("FEATURE_ID", "spcode"), all.x = TRUE ) all2 <- all[ , .(PRJ_VOL_DWB_conifer = sum(vol, na.rm = TRUE)), by = c("FEATURE_ID", "PRJ_TOTAL_AGE") ] return(all2) } 5.3.2.2 Extract species codes Convert species fields into long format. d <- unique( yld[, .( FEATURE_ID, SPECIES_1_CODE, SPECIES_2_CODE, SPECIES_3_CODE, SPECIES_4_CODE, SPECIES_5_CODE, SPECIES_6_CODE )] ) d2 <- melt( d, id.vars = "FEATURE_ID", measure.vars = c( "SPECIES_1_CODE", "SPECIES_2_CODE", "SPECIES_3_CODE", "SPECIES_4_CODE", "SPECIES_5_CODE", "SPECIES_6_CODE" ), variable.name = "spcode", value.name = "sp" ) 5.3.2.3 Extract projected volumes e <- unique( yld[, .( FEATURE_ID, PRJ_TOTAL_AGE, PRJ_SP1_VOL_DWB, PRJ_SP2_VOL_DWB, PRJ_SP3_VOL_DWB, PRJ_SP4_VOL_DWB, PRJ_SP5_VOL_DWB, PRJ_SP6_VOL_DWB )] ) t <- unique( yld[, .( FEATURE_ID, PRJ_TOTAL_AGE, PRJ_VOL_DWB )] ) 5.3.2.4 Calculate conifer and deciduous Volume f <- NoDecidVol_calculator( sp_list = d2, vol_list = e, decidsp = decid ) final <- t %>% left_join(f, by = join_by(FEATURE_ID, PRJ_TOTAL_AGE)) %>% mutate( PRJ_VOL_DWB = round(PRJ_VOL_DWB, 2), PRJ_VOL_DWB_conifer = round(PRJ_VOL_DWB_conifer, 2), PRJ_VOL_DWB_decid = PRJ_VOL_DWB - coalesce(PRJ_VOL_DWB_conifer, 0) ) #convert to wide format final_wide <- final %>% mutate( age_label = paste0("_", PRJ_TOTAL_AGE) ) %>% pivot_wider( id_cols = FEATURE_ID, names_from = age_label, values_from = c( PRJ_VOL_DWB_conifer, PRJ_VOL_DWB_decid ), names_glue = "{ifelse(.value == 'PRJ_VOL_DWB_conifer', 'MVcon', 'MVdec')}{age_label}" ) 5.3.3 Separate conifer height and deciduous height 5.3.3.1 Height ratios The following develops a set of BEC-specific conifer-to-deciduous dominant height ratios that are subsequently used to separate VDYP projected dominant heights into conifer and deciduous components for the provincial yield table. The workflow begins by connecting to the provincial MSYT PostgreSQL database and loading the provincial managed stand yield dataset. From the MSYT outputs, only features containing valid conifer (HTcon_120) and deciduous (HTdec_120) dominant heights at age 120 are retained. These features are then joined to their corresponding Biogeoclimatic Ecosystem Classification (BEC) zone and subzone information, creating a unique BEC label for each feature. For every stand, the script calculates both the deciduous-to-conifer height ratio (HTdec_120 / HTcon_120) and the conifer-to-deciduous height ratio (HTcon_120 / HTdec_120). These ratios are then summarized by BEC zone and subzone, producing average height relationships for each ecological unit based on all available managed stand observations. The resulting table contains the number of contributing samples and mean height ratios for each BEC variant and is exported as bec_height_ratios.csv. These ratios are subsequently used within the provincial NSYT workflow to estimate separate conifer and deciduous dominant height projections from a single VDYP dominant height prediction, ensuring consistency between the provincial managed stand and natural stand yield datasets. ibrary(DBI) library(RPostgreSQL) library(tidyverse) library(tidytext) library(arsenal) library(dlookr) library(fs) Chapter 6 Comparison with VRI R1 6.1 Get data 6.2 Closest Match

Chapter 6 Comparison with VRI R1 The comparison process was designed to assess the consistency between the Beta version of the 2026 Provincial NSYT dataset and the currently published VRI R1. The workflow begins by loading the provincial NSYT yield table and joining it to the R1 inventory database using a common feature_id. Species, age, and volume information from both sources are combined to create a common analytical framework. To facilitate a direct comparison, stand ages are standardized, volume estimates are derived from the R1 inventory, and VDYP yield projections are interpolated to match the specific ages represented in the inventory. This allows both datasets to be evaluated using comparable volume estimates for the same forest features. A difference metric is calculated to quantify the variation between the inventory-derived volume (volume from VRI R1 layer) and the NSYT volume estimate (interpolated NSYT volume). The resulting dataset provides a feature-by-feature comparison of the two yield systems and supports statistical evaluation of differences across the provincial land base. This process serves as a validation and quality assurance exercise, helping identify areas where modelling assumptions, age treatments, interpolation methods, or source inventory data may contribute to differences between the existing R1 inventory volumes and the proposed provincial NSYT yield dataset. The following contrasts the Beta version of the 2026 NSYT dataset with the currently published production year 2025 (projection year Dec 2024) veg_comp_lyr_r1_poly_2024 (R1). The R1 contains 6872386 features, 90% of which (6209645) has a species identified and (spec_cd_1) and 89% (6140967) remain after initial cleaning. library(DBI) library(RPostgreSQL) library(tidyverse) library(terra) library(R.utils) library(tidytext) library(arsenal) library(dlookr) library(gdalUtilities) library(glue) library(janitor) library(knitr) library(kableExtra) library(tidytext) library(fs) library(data.table) library(glue) library(janitor) library(dplyr) rm(list = ls()) options(scipen = 999) set.seed(123) # source("log_files.R") knitr::opts_chunk$set( echo = TRUE, # Show code in the output fig.width = 4, # Set the width of the figures (in inches) fig.height = 4, # Set the height of the figures (in inches) collapse = TRUE, # Collapsing code and output # out.width = '250px', # Standardize output image width # out.height = '250px', # Standardize output image height message = FALSE, # Hide messages warning = FALSE # Hide warnings ) 6.1 Get data The following snippet loads 2026 provincial NSYT from an RDS file containing 6140967 features, and joins it with the data pulled from the VRI vegetation composition R1 layer based on feature_id. Various new columns are created, including volumes from VRI R1 layer(vri_vol), capped age (vri_age250), and interpolated volumes (vdyp_vol) based on age ranges. schema <- 'msyt_2025' opt <- paste0("-c search_path=",schema) user_name <- 'results' database <- 'msyt' db<- dbConnect('PostgreSQL',dbname=database,user=user_name,options = opt) prov<-readRDS("./data/rds/prov_nsyt2026.rds") sql<-glue_sql("select feature_id, proj_age_1 as age, species_cd_1 as leading_species, species_cd_2 as second_species, live_stand_volume_125 as r1_vol125, live_stand_volume_175 as r1_vol175, dead_stand_volume_125 as r1_dvol125 from veg_comp_fdw_full",.con = db) r1<-dbGetQuery(db,sql) ############################################################################## # # prov_df<-inner_join(prov_df,r1,join_by("feature_id")) prov_df2 <- prov_df %>% mutate( vri_vol = case_when( grepl("^P", leading_species) ~ r1_vol125, grepl("^A", leading_species) ~ r1_vol125, grepl("^E", leading_species) ~ r1_vol125, grepl("^D", leading_species) ~ r1_vol125, grepl("^M", leading_species) ~ r1_vol125, grepl("^W", leading_species) ~ r1_vol125, TRUE ~ r1_vol175 # Otherwise, assign r1_vol175 ), across(where(is.numeric), ~replace_na(., 0)), vri_age250 = pmin(age, 250), age_low = floor(vri_age250/10) * 10, age_high = ceiling(vri_age250/10) * 10, p_low = (age_high - vri_age250) / 10 ) %>% group_by(feature_id) %>% mutate( l_vol = prj_vol_dwb[match(age_low, prj_total_age)], u_vol = prj_vol_dwb[match(age_high, prj_total_age)], vdyp_vol = (p_low * l_vol) + ((1 - p_low) * u_vol) ) %>% select(feature_id, vri_vol,l_vol, u_vol, vdyp_vol)%>% slice(1)%>% ungroup() nsyt<-inner_join(prov_df,prov_df2,join_by("feature_id")) 6.2 Closest Match The closest match volume calculation works by identifying the record with the smallest absolute difference between two volume estimates for each feature. Filtering for Valid Volumes: The script first filters out any rows where either vdyp_vol or prj_vol_dwb (the decadal VDYP volume) is NA, ensuring that only valid rows with both volume estimates are considered for comparison. Grouping by feature_id: The data is grouped by feature_id, meaning the script performs the closest match calculation separately for each feature (or record). Identifying the Closest Match: For each group (feature_id), the script calculates the absolute difference between vdyp_vol and prj_vol_dwb using the expression abs(prj_vol_dwb - vdyp_vol). The row with the smallest difference is considered the “closest match” for that feature. This is achieved using the slice_min function, which selects the row where the absolute difference is the smallest (n = 1), ensuring that ties (rows with the same smallest difference) are excluded (with_ties = FALSE). Calculating the Volume Difference: Once the closest match is found, a new column vol_dif is created, which represents the difference between the VDYP derived lineral interpolation and the R1 volume: vol_dif = vri_vol - vdyp_vol.The output is then saved as a RDS file. The resulting NSYT closest data frame is loaded to disk and its vol_dif field is then assessed statistically using the dlookr::describe function. nsyt_closest <- nsyt %>% filter(!is.na(vdyp_vol), !is.na(prj_vol_dwb)) %>% group_by(feature_id) %>% slice_min(abs(prj_vol_dwb - vdyp_vol), n = 1, with_ties = FALSE) %>% ungroup()%>% mutate(vol_dif = vri_vol - vdyp_vol) (#tab:tab-closest_match)Volume features assessed the mean difference in volume between the VDYP dataset and the R1 is -1.8m3 (sd: 11.13), the median difference is -0.015m3 , 986984 (16%) of the features have volume difference > +/- 5m3, 454585 (8%) features have volume difference > +/- 10m3 and 170037 (2.7%) features have volume difference > +/- 20m3. The are a number of sources that drive divergence including approaches to interpolation (most common source for volume differences < 5m3), age cutoffs and layer/project table input error in both the R1 and vdyp_vol dataset. The provincial VDYP dataset has an age cutoff of 250 years effecting 244710 (25%) of the 986984 of the features have volume difference > +/- 5m3. 85% of the features > 250 years of age have vdyp volumes > the R1 volume suggesting the much older stands in the R1 are decrementing volume, driving the divergence. Chapter 7 comparison with combined mulitple layers’ projection As noted the provincial 2026 NSYT process generates yield projections using only the Rank 1 (R1) forest layer from each VRI polygon. This approach was adopted to support efficient province-wide yield generation while maintaining consistency with the leading stand layer that typically represents the majority of forest volume and site productivity within a polygon. However, many VRI polygons contain multiple forest layers, such as a primary overstory layer combined with residual, secondary, or understory layers. In a standard VDYP7 workflow, each eligible layer can be projected independently, and the resulting layer-level yields are subsequently aggregated to produce a polygon-level yield projection. As a result, differences may arise between projections generated using only the R1 layer and projections generated by combining all forested layers within a polygon. This chapter evaluates the magnitude and significance of those differences by comparing the provincial NSYT “R1-only” projections against yield projections derived from the full multi-layer representation of VRI polygons. The analysis examines two distinct polygon types: (1) polygons containing a primary (P) layer and one or more additional live forest layers, and (2) polygons containing only a primary layer with one dead (D) layer. By comparing projected merchantable volume through time for both approaches, the assessment quantifies the effects of simplifying polygon representation to a single layer and evaluates whether the resulting provincial yield estimates remain representative of the yields that would be obtained using the complete multi-layer inventory structure. The results provide an important validation of the NSYT methodology and help identify circumstances where multi-layer stand dynamics may contribute meaningfully to differences in projected yield.
